title: Forgotten Realms
summary: The most popular and widely used D&D campaign setting, a high-fantasy world of magic, diverse cultures, and epic adventures set on the world of Toril.
content:
## Forgotten Realms

**Also Known As:** Faerûn (primary continent), Toril (the world), Abeir-Toril
**Created By:** Ed Greenwood
**First Appeared:** 1987 (campaign setting box set)
**Default Setting For:** D&D 5th Edition
**Tone:** High Fantasy, Sword and Sorcery

### Overview

The Forgotten Realms is the default setting for D&D 5th Edition and the most extensively detailed campaign world in the game's history. Originally created by Ed Greenwood as a setting for his childhood stories, it became a published D&D setting in 1987 and has since been the backdrop for hundreds of novels, video games (Baldur's Gate, Neverwinter Nights, Icewind Dale), and published adventures.

The setting is characterized by a rich tapestry of cultures, powerful magic, active gods, and a deep history spanning tens of thousands of years. It is a world where almost any kind of D&D adventure can take place.

### Major Regions

- **Sword Coast:** The most adventured region in all of D&D. A stretch of western Faerûn along the Sea of Swords, featuring major cities and wild frontier lands. Most 5e adventures take place here.
- **Waterdeep:** The City of Splendors, the greatest city on the Sword Coast. A massive, cosmopolitan metropolis ruled by masked Lords. Home to the famous Yawning Portal tavern, which sits atop the mega-dungeon Undermountain.
- **Baldur's Gate:** A sprawling, corrupt port city and a major mercantile hub. The setting of the famous video game series and the adventure *Descent into Avernus*.
- **Neverwinter:** The Jewel of the North, a city rebuilding after a volcanic eruption. A frequent starting point for adventures.
- **Icewind Dale:** The frozen far north, home to Ten-Towns and the setting of *Rime of the Frostmaiden*. A harsh, isolated wilderness.
- **Underdark:** A vast subterranean network of caverns beneath Faerûn, home to drow, mind flayers, beholders, and other horrors. The setting of *Out of the Abyss*.
- **Calimshan:** A desert realm inspired by Arabian and Middle Eastern cultures, home to genies and ancient magic.
- **Thay:** A magocracy ruled by the lich Szass Tam and the Red Wizards, a major source of villainy in the Realms.
- **Cormyr:** The Forest Kingdom, a classic feudal realm of knights and nobility.
- **Amn:** A wealthy merchant nation south of the Sword Coast.

### Major Organizations

- **Harpers:** A secret network of spellcasters and spies who work to promote good and maintain the balance of power. Symbol: a silver harp.
- **Zhentarim (Black Network):** A mercenary organization that seeks to control trade and accumulate wealth and power. Morally gray at best.
- **Lords' Alliance:** A coalition of rulers from cities across the Sword Coast who band together for mutual security and prosperity.
- **Emerald Enclave:** Druids, rangers, and others who oppose threats to the natural world and help others survive the wilderness.
- **Order of the Gauntlet:** A devoted group of faithful and vigilant seekers of justice who protect others from the depredations of evildoers.

### Key Historical Events

- **The Time of Troubles (1358 DR):** The gods were forced to walk the mortal world, some died, and the nature of divine magic was forever changed.
- **The Spellplague (1385 DR):** The goddess of magic was killed, causing widespread magical catastrophe that reshaped the world.
- **The Sundering (1482–1487 DR):** The world was restored to its pre-Spellplague state, establishing the status quo for 5th Edition.

### Key NPCs

- **Elminster Aumar:** The Sage of Shadowdale, one of the most powerful wizards in the Realms and a Chosen of Mystra.
- **Drizzt Do'Urden:** The famous drow ranger who forsook the evil of his homeland to become a hero of the surface world.
- **Laeral Silverhand:** The Open Lord of Waterdeep and one of the Seven Sisters, Chosen of Mystra.
- **Szass Tam:** The lich who rules Thay, one of the most powerful undead beings in the Realms.

### Published 5e Adventures Set Here

- Lost Mine of Phandelver (Starter Set)
- Hoard of the Dragon Queen / Rise of Tiamat
- Princes of the Apocalypse
- Out of the Abyss
- Storm King's Thunder
- Tomb of Annihilation (Chult)
- Waterdeep: Dragon Heist
- Waterdeep: Dungeon of the Mad Mage
- Baldur's Gate: Descent into Avernus
- Icewind Dale: Rime of the Frostmaiden

### What Makes It Unique

The Forgotten Realms is the "kitchen sink" setting — it has a place for virtually every type of fantasy adventure. Its depth of published lore is unmatched, with thousands of named NPCs, locations, and historical events. This can be both a strength (endless inspiration) and a challenge (keeping track of canon). For new DMs, the Sword Coast is the easiest place to start.
tags: setting,forgotten realms,faerûn,toril,sword coast,waterdeep,baldur's gate,high fantasy,5e default

title: Greyhawk
summary: The original D&D campaign setting created by Gary Gygax, a gritty sword-and-sorcery world that served as the default setting for earlier editions.
content:
## Greyhawk

**Also Known As:** The World of Greyhawk, Oerth
**Created By:** Gary Gygax
**First Appeared:** 1975 (in play), 1980 (published)
**Default Setting For:** D&D 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Editions; 2024 D&D revision
**Tone:** Gritty Sword and Sorcery, Classic Fantasy

### Overview

Greyhawk is the original D&D campaign setting, born from Gary Gygax's home game. It is a more grounded, sword-and-sorcery-flavored world compared to the high magic of the Forgotten Realms. Magic is rarer and more mysterious, and the tone is darker and more morally ambiguous. Many of the most iconic D&D adventures — Temple of Elemental Evil, Tomb of Horrors, Against the Giants — were originally set in Greyhawk.

Greyhawk returned to prominence with the 2024 D&D rules revision, which uses it as the default setting.

### The World of Oerth

The planet is called Oerth, and the primary continent of play is called the **Flanaess**, a region roughly analogous to medieval Europe. Unlike the Forgotten Realms, Greyhawk has large areas of unexplored wilderness, ancient ruins, and a frontier feel.

### Major Regions and Locations

- **Free City of Greyhawk:** The central hub, a large independent city-state near the famous Castle Greyhawk dungeon (Gygax's original mega-dungeon).
- **The Wild Coast:** A rough, lawless stretch of shoreline filled with pirates and adventurers.
- **The Great Kingdom (Aerdy):** Once the largest empire on Oerth, now fractured and ruled by a mad Overking.
- **Furyondy and Veluna:** Good-aligned kingdoms that stand against the forces of evil.
- **Iuz:** An empire ruled by the demigod Iuz the Old, a half-demon tyrant.
- **The Pomarj:** Orc and humanoid-controlled territory, a constant threat.
- **The Barrier Peaks:** Home to a crashed spaceship (yes, really — see *Expedition to the Barrier Peaks*).
- **The Temple of Elemental Evil:** One of the most famous dungeon complexes in D&D history.

### Key NPCs

- **Mordenkainen:** A powerful archmage who seeks to maintain the Balance between good and evil. Creator of many iconic spells (Mordenkainen's Faithful Hound, Magnificent Mansion, etc.).
- **Tenser:** Another archmage, creator of Tenser's Floating Disk. More actively good-aligned than Mordenkainen.
- **Iuz the Old:** A demigod of evil, son of the demon prince Graz'zt and the witch Iggwilv.
- **Vecna:** The lich-god of secrets, who ascended to godhood. Origin of the Hand and Eye of Vecna.
- **Rary:** A wizard who betrayed the Circle of Eight.

### Classic Adventures Set Here

- The Temple of Elemental Evil (T1-4)
- Against the Giants (G1-3)
- Tomb of Horrors (S1)
- White Plume Mountain (S2)
- Expedition to the Barrier Peaks (S3)
- Descent into the Depths of the Earth (D1-3)
- Queen of the Demonweb Pits (Q1)
- The Village of Hommlet (T1)

### What Makes It Unique

Greyhawk has a distinctly old-school feel. Magic is less common, alignments matter more, and the world has a gritty, dangerous edge. It's the setting where D&D itself was born, and many game mechanics (named spells like Bigby's Hand, Tasha's Hideous Laughter) reference Greyhawk characters. It rewards careful, strategic play and has a strong "dungeon crawling" tradition.

title: Dragonlance
summary: A world shaped by the War of the Lance, where dragonarmies threaten civilization and heroes wield legendary Dragonlances against chromatic dragons.
content:
## Dragonlance

**Also Known As:** Krynn
**Created By:** Tracy Hickman and Margaret Weis (with Laura Hickman)
**First Appeared:** 1984
**Tone:** Epic Fantasy, War Story, Redemption

### Overview

Dragonlance is a setting defined by its epic narrative. Set on the world of Krynn, the setting tells the story of the War of the Lance, in which the forces of the evil dragon goddess Takhisis (Tiamat by another name) unleash dragonarmies upon the world. Heroes must rediscover the lost gods of good, forge the legendary Dragonlances, and rally the free peoples to fight back.

The setting is deeply tied to its novel series — the *Dragonlance Chronicles* and *Dragonlance Legends* trilogies by Weis and Hickman are among the best-selling fantasy novels ever written.

### Major Regions and Locations

- **Ansalon:** The primary continent, divided into diverse kingdoms and wilderness.
- **Solace:** The famous town built in giant vallenwood trees, home of the Inn of the Last Home.
- **Palanthas:** The greatest city on Ansalon, home to the Tower of High Sorcery.
- **Tarsis:** A former port city, now land-locked after the Cataclysm.
- **The Blood Sea of Istar:** A massive whirlpool where the holy city of Istar once stood, destroyed by the Cataclysm.
- **Neraka:** Stronghold of the dragonarmies and Takhisis's forces.
- **Mount Nevermind:** Home of the tinker gnomes, filled with bizarre and often malfunctioning inventions.

### Unique Races

- **Kender:** Small, fearless, endlessly curious folk who "borrow" things without meaning to steal. Often found with objects they don't remember picking up. Beloved and infuriating in equal measure.
- **Tinker Gnomes:** Obsessed with invention and technology, creating elaborate (and often comically dysfunctional) machines.
- **Draconians:** Dragon-people created by corrupting good dragon eggs. Serve the dragonarmies.
- **Irda:** Beautiful, reclusive ogre descendants who practice powerful magic.

### The Gods of Krynn

The gods are organized into three groups:
- **Gods of Good:** Paladine (Bahamut), Mishakal, Kiri-Jolith, and others.
- **Gods of Neutrality:** Gilean, Sirrion, Reorx, and others.
- **Gods of Evil:** Takhisis (Tiamat), Sargonnas, Chemosh, and others.

The gods withdrew from Krynn after the Kingpriest of Istar demanded divine power, causing the Cataclysm (a mountain dropped from the sky). Rediscovering the true gods is a central theme.

### Key NPCs

- **Tanis Half-Elven:** Leader of the Heroes of the Lance, torn between his human and elven heritage.
- **Raistlin Majere:** The brilliant, ambitious, sickly mage who may be the most powerful wizard ever — and the most dangerous.
- **Goldmoon:** Barbarian princess who rediscovered the gods of good.
- **Sturm Brightblade:** A knight who embodied honor and sacrifice.
- **Tasslehoff Burrfoot:** The most famous kender in fiction, irrepressible and brave.
- **Lord Soth:** A death knight, once a noble Knight of Solamnia who fell to darkness.

### Key Historical Events

- **The Cataclysm:** The gods hurled a fiery mountain at Istar, reshaping the world and withdrawing from mortals.
- **The War of the Lance:** Takhisis's dragonarmies invade, the Dragonlances are reforged, and the heroes save the world.
- **The Chaos War:** The god of chaos threatens all of Krynn.

### Published 5e Material

- *Dragonlance: Shadow of the Dragon Queen* (2022 adventure)
- *Dragonlance: Warriors of Krynn* (board game tie-in)

### What Makes It Unique

Dragonlance is the most story-driven of the classic settings. It has a defined narrative arc, iconic characters, and a strong emphasis on themes of heroism, sacrifice, and redemption. The setting's tight integration with its novels gives it an emotional resonance that few other settings match. It also features unique mechanical elements like the Dragonlance weapon, the Orders of High Sorcery (wizards must take a test and join an order), and the Solamnic Knights' code of honor.

title: Eberron
summary: A magitech world recovering from a devastating war, blending noir intrigue, pulp adventure, and wide magic with dragonmarks, warforged, and lightning rail.
content:
## Eberron

**Also Known As:** The World of Eberron, Khorvaire
**Created By:** Keith Baker
**First Appeared:** 2004 (won the WotC setting search contest)
**Tone:** Noir, Pulp Adventure, Magitech, Intrigue

### Overview

Eberron is a setting that asks "what if magic were an industrial force?" Rather than a medieval world with magic sprinkled on top, Eberron imagines a society where low-level magic is commonplace and integrated into everyday life. Trains run on lightning rails, communication happens through sending stones, and newspapers are printed with magic. The world is recovering from the devastating Last War, which ended when an entire nation was mysteriously destroyed in a magical cataclysm called the Mourning.

The setting is deliberately designed so that any alignment can belong to any race or organization. Good-aligned undead exist. Evil paladins are common. Nothing is as it seems.

### Major Regions and Locations

- **Khorvaire:** The primary continent, home to the Five Nations and the setting for most adventures.
- **Sharn, the City of Towers:** A vertical city of impossibly tall towers, connected by bridges and skycoaches. The most common adventure hub in Eberron, perfect for noir-style urban adventures.
- **The Mournland:** The blasted, dead-magic wasteland where the nation of Cyre once stood. No one knows what caused the Mourning. Living spells, mutated creatures, and twisted landscapes fill this horror zone.
- **Stormreach:** A frontier city on the continent of Xen'drik, built atop giant ruins.
- **Droaam:** A nation of monsters — medusas, hags, and ogres — run by the Daughters of Sora Kell.
- **The Talenta Plains:** Home to halflings who ride dinosaurs.
- **Darguun:** A goblinoid nation.
- **Karrnath:** A militaristic nation that used undead soldiers in the Last War.

### Unique Features

- **Dragonmarks:** Magical marks that appear on members of specific bloodlines, granting supernatural abilities. The Dragonmarked Houses are the most powerful economic forces in Khorvaire, controlling everything from banking (House Kundarak) to transportation (House Orien) to healing (House Jorasco).
- **Warforged:** Sentient constructs built as soldiers for the Last War, now struggling to find their identity as free beings. A playable race unique to Eberron.
- **The Lightning Rail:** A magical train network connecting major cities across Khorvaire.
- **Artificers:** The Artificer class was designed for Eberron, reflecting its magitech themes.
- **The Last War:** A century-long conflict that shattered the once-united Kingdom of Galifar into five rival nations. The war ended, but peace is fragile.

### Major Organizations

- **The Twelve Dragonmarked Houses:** Powerful mercantile dynasties that control key industries.
- **The Aurum:** A secret society of the wealthy and powerful seeking to control Khorvaire.
- **The Chamber:** An ancient organization of dragons that manipulates events according to the Draconic Prophecy.
- **The Lord of Blades:** A warforged leader who wants to create a warforged nation and sees organic races as oppressors.
- **The Dreaming Dark:** Psychic entities from the plane of dreams who secretly manipulate mortal civilizations.

### Key Historical Events

- **The Last War (894–996 YK):** A century-long civil war that broke the Kingdom of Galifar into five nations.
- **The Day of Mourning (994 YK):** Cyre was destroyed in a single day by an unknown magical catastrophe, creating the Mournland. This event ended the war — everyone was terrified it could happen again.
- **The Treaty of Thronehold (996 YK):** The peace treaty that officially ended the Last War and granted warforged their freedom.

### Published 5e Material

- *Eberron: Rising from the Last War* (2019, full campaign setting)
- *Wayfinder's Guide to Eberron* (DMs Guild)

### What Makes It Unique

Eberron breaks D&D conventions. Its magitech aesthetic, noir tone, and moral ambiguity set it apart from traditional fantasy. The mystery of the Mourning provides an overarching campaign hook, and the political intrigue of the post-war period gives DMs endless material. Keith Baker's guiding principle — "if it exists in D&D, there's a place for it in Eberron" — means all D&D content can fit, but the setting gives everything a unique twist.

title: Ravenloft
summary: The Domains of Dread, a horror-themed demiplane of mist-shrouded realms, each ruled by a cursed Darklord imprisoned by the enigmatic Dark Powers.
content:
## Ravenloft

**Also Known As:** The Domains of Dread, The Demiplane of Dread
**Created By:** Tracy Hickman and Laura Hickman
**First Appeared:** 1983 (module I6: Ravenloft)
**Tone:** Gothic Horror, Dark Fantasy, Psychological Horror

### Overview

Ravenloft is not a single world but a demiplane — a pocket dimension of horror created and maintained by the enigmatic Dark Powers. It consists of isolated domains, each ruled by a Darklord: a powerful villain who is both the master and the prisoner of their domain. The Mists of Ravenloft can reach into any world and pull unsuspecting travelers into the Domains of Dread.

The setting is designed for horror campaigns, drawing on Gothic literature, vampire fiction, werewolf tales, cosmic horror, and psychological thriller traditions.

### The Nature of the Domains

- Each domain is surrounded by the **Mists**, which prevent escape and can shift, merge, or isolate domains.
- Each domain is ruled by a **Darklord** — a being who committed a great evil and was drawn into Ravenloft as both ruler and prisoner.
- The **Dark Powers** are the unknowable force that created the Demiplane. Their motives are mysterious. They empower the Darklords but also ensure their eternal torment.
- Domains can be as small as a single castle or as large as an entire continent.

### Notable Domains and Darklords

- **Barovia (Darklord: Count Strahd von Zarovich):** The most famous domain. A fog-shrouded valley ruled by the vampire Strahd, who is cursed to eternally pursue the reincarnation of his lost love, Tatyana. The setting of the adventure *Curse of Strahd*.
- **Lamordia (Darklord: Viktra Mordenheim):** Inspired by Frankenstein. A domain of mad science and flesh golems.
- **Darkon (Darklord: Azalin Rex):** A large domain ruled by a powerful lich-king who cannot learn new magic.
- **Falkovnia (Darklord: Vladeska Drakov):** A military dictatorship besieged by undead hordes.
- **Har'Akir (Darklord: Ankhtepot):** An Egyptian-themed domain ruled by a mummy lord.
- **Kartakass:** A domain of werewolves and bards, where music holds both beauty and danger.
- **Dementlieu:** A domain of masquerade and illusion, inspired by French aristocratic horror.
- **I'Cath:** A domain inspired by Chinese folklore, where a Darklord built a perfect city that exists only in dreams.

### Horror Genres by Domain

Ravenloft supports many horror subgenres:
- **Gothic Horror:** Barovia, Dementlieu
- **Body Horror:** Lamordia
- **Cosmic Horror:** Bluetspur (mind flayer domain)
- **Dark Fantasy:** Darkon
- **Ghost Story:** Mordent
- **Folk Horror:** Kartakass, Tepest
- **Disaster Horror:** Falkovnia

### Key NPCs

- **Count Strahd von Zarovich:** The first vampire, lord of Barovia, and one of the most iconic villains in D&D history.
- **Azalin Rex:** A lich and former Oerthian wizard, Strahd's rival and reluctant neighbor.
- **Rudolph van Richten:** A legendary monster hunter, the Van Helsing of Ravenloft.
- **Ezmerelda d'Avenir:** Van Richten's former protégé and a skilled monster hunter in her own right.

### Running Horror in D&D

Ravenloft introduces special rules for horror campaigns:
- **Stress and Madness:** Prolonged exposure to horror can cause lasting psychological effects.
- **Dark Gifts:** The Dark Powers may offer characters power at a terrible cost.
- **Resurrection restrictions:** Coming back from the dead is harder and may have consequences.
- **Lineages:** Characters can become dhampir, hexblood, or reborn — transformed by horror.

### Published 5e Material

- *Curse of Strahd* (2016, widely considered the best 5e adventure)
- *Van Richten's Guide to Ravenloft* (2021, full setting book)

### What Makes It Unique

Ravenloft turns D&D's usual power fantasy on its head. Characters are vulnerable, the environment is hostile, and the villains are tragic figures trapped in their own punishment. The Mists allow DMs to pull characters from any setting into Ravenloft, making it a versatile tool for any campaign. *Curse of Strahd* alone has made Ravenloft one of the most beloved settings in 5th Edition.

title: Dark Sun
summary: A brutal, post-apocalyptic desert world where arcane magic has ravaged the environment, gods are absent, psionics are common, and survival is the greatest challenge.
content:
## Dark Sun

**Also Known As:** Athas
**Created By:** Troy Denning and Timothy Brown
**First Appeared:** 1991
**Tone:** Post-Apocalyptic, Survival, Sword and Sandal, Grimdark

### Overview

Dark Sun is the most radically different of the classic D&D settings. Set on the dying world of Athas, it presents a post-apocalyptic desert landscape where reckless use of arcane magic (called defiling) has stripped the world of its oceans, forests, and fertility. There are no gods — divine magic comes from the elemental planes or the spirits of the land. Metal is scarce (most weapons are made of bone, obsidian, or chitin). Water is more valuable than gold. And the world is ruled by immortal, immensely powerful sorcerer-kings.

### The World of Athas

- **No Gods:** The gods either never existed or have abandoned Athas. Clerics draw power from the elemental planes (Earth, Air, Fire, Water) or from the paraelements (Sun, Rain, Silt, Magma).
- **No Metal:** Iron and steel are extremely rare. Most weapons and armor are made from bone, obsidian, stone, or chitin from giant insects.
- **Defiling Magic:** Arcane casters can "defile" — drawing life energy from the land to power their spells, leaving dead ash behind. This is why Athas is a wasteland. Preservers cast more carefully, taking only what the land can spare.
- **Psionics Everywhere:** Nearly every creature on Athas has some psionic ability. Psionics replace much of what magic does in other settings.
- **Slavery Is Everywhere:** The city-states are built on slave labor, and most adventures involve themes of oppression and rebellion.

### Major Regions and Locations

- **The Tablelands:** The habitable region where the city-states are located, surrounded by endless desert.
- **Tyr:** The Free City, where a slave revolt overthrew the sorcerer-king Kalak. The most common starting point for adventures.
- **Urik:** A militaristic city-state ruled by Hamanu, the Lion King.
- **Raam:** A city in chaos, its sorcerer-queen losing control.
- **Nibenay:** The Shadow City, ruled by a mysterious and reclusive sorcerer-king.
- **The Sea of Silt:** A vast expanse of powdery silt that replaced the ocean, navigated by silt skimmers.
- **The Forest Ridge:** One of the last forested areas, home to halfling cannibals.
- **The Ringing Mountains:** A massive mountain range forming the western border of the Tablelands.

### The Sorcerer-Kings

Athas is ruled by immortal tyrants who are both powerful defilers and psionicists. They have ruled for thousands of years and maintain control through brutal force and the templars — their bureaucratic enforcers who channel divine-like power from their patron sorcerer-king.

### Unique Races

- **Mul:** Half-dwarf, half-human. Bred as slave laborers, incredibly strong and tough.
- **Thri-kreen:** Mantis-folk. Four arms, no need for sleep, alien psychology.
- **Half-Giant:** Enormous, simple-minded people who shift alignment frequently.
- **Aarakocra:** Bird-people of the mountains.
- Elves in Dark Sun are tall, nomadic desert runners — con artists and traders, not the graceful forest-dwellers of other settings.
- Halflings are feral jungle-dwelling cannibals.

### Key Historical Events

- **The Cleansing Wars:** The sorcerer-kings (originally Champions of Rajaat) waged genocide against non-human races, devastating the world.
- **The Death of Borys:** The Dragon of Tyr, the most powerful being on Athas, was eventually slain.
- **The Freeing of Tyr:** Kalak was overthrown by a slave rebellion, creating the only free city.

### What Makes It Unique

Dark Sun inverts nearly every D&D assumption. There are no gods, no metal, no easy healing, and no comfortable inn to rest at. Survival is a genuine challenge. The setting deals with mature themes — slavery, ecological destruction, tyranny, and rebellion. It produces some of the most intense and memorable D&D campaigns because the stakes are always life and death. Characters are tougher (traditionally starting at higher levels) because they have to be.

### Published Material

Dark Sun has not received an official 5e sourcebook as of this writing, but it has extensive 2e and 4e material. It remains one of the most requested settings for a 5e release.

title: Planescape
summary: A setting centered on Sigil, the City of Doors, at the nexus of the multiverse, where philosophical factions vie for influence and portals lead to every plane of existence.
content:
## Planescape

**Also Known As:** The Planes, Sigil and the Outlands
**Created By:** Zeb Cook (with Dana Knutson's visual design)
**First Appeared:** 1994
**Tone:** Philosophical, Weird Fantasy, Urban, Multiversal

### Overview

Planescape is D&D's multiverse setting. Rather than being set on a single world, it encompasses the entire Great Wheel cosmology — the Inner Planes, the Outer Planes, the Astral and Ethereal Planes, and the strange spaces in between. At the center of it all sits Sigil, the City of Doors, a torus-shaped city floating atop an infinitely tall spire at the center of the Outlands.

Planescape is a setting where belief shapes reality, philosophy is power, and the answer to "what is the meaning of existence?" might literally determine the fate of a plane.

### Sigil, the City of Doors

- **The Cage:** Sigil is a city built on the inside of a giant ring (torus), floating above the Outlands. Look up and you see the other side of the city.
- **Portals Everywhere:** Sigil contains portals to every plane of existence. Any archway, window, or doorframe might be a portal — if you have the right key (which could be anything: a specific item, a thought, a word).
- **The Lady of Pain:** Sigil's enigmatic protector. She is not a god (gods cannot enter Sigil). She floats silently through the streets, and those who worship her or displease her are flayed or cast into deadly extradimensional mazes. She ensures no faction gains total control.
- **No Gods Allowed:** Deities are barred from Sigil, making it neutral ground in cosmic conflicts.

### The Factions

Sigil is politically divided among philosophical factions, each embodying a belief about the nature of the multiverse:

- **The Athar (Defiers):** The gods are frauds; true divinity lies beyond them.
- **The Believers of the Source (Godsmen):** All beings have the potential to become divine through testing and growth.
- **The Bleak Cabal (Bleakers):** The multiverse has no meaning; find your own.
- **The Doomguard:** Entropy is the natural state; decay and destruction are the ultimate purpose.
- **The Dustmen:** Everyone is already dead; true death is the release from this existence.
- **The Fated (Takers):** The multiverse belongs to those strong enough to take what they want.
- **The Fraternity of Order (Guvners):** The multiverse runs on laws and rules; understanding them is power.
- **The Free League (Indeps):** No faction has the right answer; think for yourself.
- **The Harmonium (Hardheads):** Peace and harmony through law and order — by force if necessary.
- **The Mercykillers:** Justice is absolute; the guilty must be punished.
- **The Revolutionary League (Anarchists):** All factions and institutions are corrupt; tear them down.
- **The Sign of One (Signers):** The multiverse exists because you imagine it; you are the center.
- **The Society of Sensation (Sensates):** Experience everything; the multiverse is known through the senses.
- **The Transcendent Order (Ciphers):** Action without thought; become one with the multiverse through instinct.
- **The Xaositects (Chaosmen):** Chaos is truth; embrace randomness.

### The Planes (Great Wheel)

- **Inner Planes:** Elemental Planes (Fire, Water, Earth, Air) and their combinations.
- **Outer Planes:** 16 planes arranged in a wheel by alignment (Mount Celestia for LG, the Abyss for CE, Mechanus for LN, Limbo for CN, etc.).
- **Transitive Planes:** The Astral Plane (connects to Outer Planes), the Ethereal Plane (connects to Inner Planes), and the Shadowfell/Feywild.
- **The Outlands:** The neutral center of the Great Wheel, where Sigil floats.

### Key NPCs

- **The Lady of Pain:** The silent, enigmatic ruler of Sigil. Not to be worshipped. Not to be challenged.
- **Shemeshka the Marauder:** An arcanaloth information broker and one of the most dangerous beings in Sigil.
- **A'kin:** A friendly arcanaloth shopkeeper. Suspiciously friendly.
- **Fell:** A dabus who was cast out for worshipping the Lady of Pain. Now a tattoo artist.

### Planescape Slang

Planescape has its own distinctive cant:
- **Berk:** A fool.
- **Blood:** A skilled or knowledgeable person.
- **The Chant:** News, rumors, information.
- **Clueless (Primer):** Someone from the Prime Material Plane who doesn't know how things work on the planes.
- **The Dark:** A secret.
- **Pike Off:** Go away (rudely).

### Published 5e Material

- *Planescape: Adventures in the Multiverse* (2023, setting book + adventures)

### What Makes It Unique

Planescape is the most intellectually ambitious D&D setting. Ideas and beliefs literally reshape reality. A faction that gains enough followers can alter the nature of a plane. The setting encourages roleplaying, philosophical debate, and creative problem-solving over combat. Its visual aesthetic — industrial fantasy designed by Tony DiTerlizzi — is instantly recognizable and unlike anything else in D&D.

title: Spelljammer
summary: D&D in space — spelljamming ships sail between crystal spheres through the phlogiston (or Astral Sea in 5e), connecting all D&D worlds in a spacefaring fantasy adventure.
content:
## Spelljammer

**Also Known As:** Wildspace, D&D in Space
**Created By:** Jeff Grubb
**First Appeared:** 1989
**Tone:** Swashbuckling, Pulp Sci-Fi Fantasy, Whimsical, Age of Sail in Space

### Overview

Spelljammer answers the question "what's between the worlds?" In this setting, the various D&D worlds (Toril, Oerth, Krynn, etc.) each exist within their own crystal sphere, and these spheres float in a medium called the phlogiston (in classic lore) or the Astral Sea (in 5e). Ships powered by magic — spelljamming helms — sail between these worlds, creating a spacefaring fantasy setting.

The tone is lighter and more whimsical than most D&D settings, with a swashbuckling, Age of Sail aesthetic transplanted into a fantastical version of outer space.

### How Spelljamming Works

- **Spelljamming Helm:** A magical throne that converts a spellcaster's magical energy into motive force for the ship. The caster sits in the helm and pilots the ship, but loses access to their spell slots while doing so.
- **Air Envelopes:** Ships carry a bubble of breathable air with them, which is refreshed by plant life on board.
- **Gravity Planes:** Ships generate their own gravity, typically pulling toward the deck.
- **Wildspace:** The space within a crystal sphere (2e) or a Wildspace system (5e).
- **The Astral Sea (5e):** The silvery void between Wildspace systems that ships travel through.
- **The Phlogiston (2e):** A flammable, rainbow-colored medium between crystal spheres. Fire magic in the phlogiston is extremely dangerous.

### Unique Ships

Spelljammer ships come in fantastical designs:
- **Nautiloid:** Mind flayer ships shaped like nautilus shells. Terrifying and powerful.
- **Hammerhead:** A versatile, hammerhead-shark-shaped ship favored by many races.
- **Man-o-War:** A wasp-shaped elven warship grown from magical plants.
- **Squid Ship:** A squid-shaped vessel, common and affordable.
- **Tyrant Ship:** Beholder ships — spherical, covered in eye-ports for their deadly gaze weapons.
- **Space Galleon:** A traditional galleon modified for spelljamming.
- **The Spelljammer:** A legendary, miles-long manta-ray-shaped living ship that is the namesake of the entire setting. Finding it is one of the great quests.

### Unique Races and Creatures

- **Giff:** Hippopotami-folk who love firearms and military organization. Endearingly bombastic.
- **Hadozee:** Gliding simian-folk (deck apes) who make excellent sailors.
- **Autognomes:** Mechanical gnome constructs.
- **Plasmoids:** Amorphous, ooze-like beings.
- **Thri-kreen:** Mantis-folk who appear as traders and wanderers in Wildspace.
- **Astral Elves:** Elves who have lived in the Astral Sea so long they've become something new.
- **Neogi:** Spidery slave-traders, universally feared and despised.
- **Space Clowns:** Yes. This setting has them. Spelljammer embraces the weird.
- **Giant Space Hamsters:** The propulsion source for gnomish spelljamming vessels. Gnomes use giant hamster wheels.

### Major Factions

- **Elven Imperial Navy:** The most powerful spacefaring fleet, policing Wildspace.
- **Mind Flayer Empire:** Nautiloid fleets that raid and enslave across Wildspace.
- **Neogi Slavers:** Spider-eel merchants who deal in slaves and cruelty.
- **The Xaryxian Empire:** An astral elf empire featured in the 5e adventure.

### Published 5e Material

- *Spelljammer: Adventures in Space* (2022, setting book + adventures + monsters)

### What Makes It Unique

Spelljammer is D&D at its most creative and playful. It connects every D&D world into a single, traversable universe, allowing characters to literally sail from the Forgotten Realms to Dragonlance. The ship-based gameplay adds a unique dimension — managing a crew, engaging in ship-to-ship combat, and exploring uncharted systems. It's the setting where you can fight mind flayers in space while your gnome engineer feeds the giant space hamster.

title: Ravnica
summary: A Magic: The Gathering crossover setting — a world-spanning city ruled by ten powerful guilds, each representing a different color combination and philosophy.
content:
## Ravnica

**Also Known As:** The City of Guilds
**Created By:** Magic: The Gathering design team (adapted for D&D by James Wyatt)
**First Appeared in D&D:** 2018
**Originally From:** Magic: The Gathering (2005)
**Tone:** Urban Fantasy, Guild Intrigue, Magical Noir

### Overview

Ravnica is a plane from Magic: The Gathering adapted for D&D play. It is an ecumenopolis — a world entirely covered by a single, sprawling city. There is no wilderness, no open ocean, no untouched nature (though parks and undercity wilds exist). Society is organized around ten guilds, each aligned with a pair of Magic's five colors of mana, and each serving a specific function in the city's operation.

The Guildpact — a magical contract — keeps the guilds in check and prevents any one from dominating the others.

### The Ten Guilds

| Guild | Colors | Role | Philosophy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Azorius Senate** | White/Blue | Lawmakers and bureaucrats | Order through law |
| **Boros Legion** | Red/White | Military and police | Justice through force |
| **Dimir** | Blue/Black | Spies and information brokers | Knowledge is power |
| **Golgari Swarm** | Black/Green | Recyclers and undercity dwellers | Life and death are one cycle |
| **Gruul Clans** | Red/Green | Anarchists and wild warriors | Civilization is a cage |
| **Izzet League** | Blue/Red | Scientists and inventors | Discovery through experimentation |
| **Orzhov Syndicate** | White/Black | Religious banking cartel | Wealth is spiritual power |
| **Rakdos Cult** | Black/Red | Entertainers and chaos agents | Freedom through hedonism |
| **Selesnya Conclave** | Green/White | Nature commune | Harmony through unity |
| **Simic Combine** | Green/Blue | Biomancers and adaptationists | Perfection through evolution |

### Major Locations

- **The Tenth District:** The primary area of play, where all ten guilds have a significant presence.
- **New Prahv:** The gleaming towers of the Azorius Senate.
- **Sunhome:** The fortress of the Boros Legion.
- **Svogthos:** The living guildhall of the Golgari, deep in the undercity.
- **Nivix:** The towering laboratory of the Izzet League.
- **Orzhova, the Church of Deals:** The cathedral of the Orzhov Syndicate.
- **Rix Maadi:** The underground lava-pit theater of the Cult of Rakdos.
- **Vitu-Ghazi:** The enormous tree-guildhall of the Selesnya Conclave.
- **The Undercity:** Miles of sewers, ruins, and forgotten layers beneath the city surface.

### Key NPCs

- **Jace Beleren:** The Living Guildpact, a planeswalker who serves as the magical arbiter between guilds.
- **Niv-Mizzet:** The ancient dragon who leads the Izzet League, obsessed with knowledge.
- **Rakdos:** A demon lord who is both the leader and the object of worship of his cult.
- **Trostani:** Three dryads fused into one being who lead the Selesnya Conclave.
- **Lazav:** The shapeshifting guildmaster of the Dimir.

### Running Ravnica

Ravnica campaigns focus on:
- **Guild intrigue and politics:** Espionage, alliances, and betrayals between guilds.
- **Urban adventure:** Investigation, heists, chases through layered cityscapes.
- **Guild identity:** Each PC belongs to a guild, giving them contacts, goals, and rivals.
- **Renown system:** Characters gain standing within their guild through deeds.

### Published 5e Material

- *Guildmasters' Guide to Ravnica* (2018)

### What Makes It Unique

Ravnica is entirely urban — there is no wilderness to explore, only layers of city. The guild system provides instant hooks, factions, and motivations for every character. It's an excellent setting for groups who enjoy political intrigue, heists, and investigations. The Magic: The Gathering crossover also introduces unique races (Loxodon, Simic Hybrids, Vedalken) and a different flavor of fantasy.

metadata_json.creator: Magic: The Gathering team (D&D adaptation by James Wyatt)

title: Theros
summary: A Greek mythology-inspired setting from Magic: The Gathering where gods walk among mortals, heroes earn divine piety, and fate is shaped by epic deeds.
content:
## Theros

**Also Known As:** The World of Theros
**Created By:** Magic: The Gathering design team (adapted for D&D by James Wyatt and F. Wesley Schneider)
**First Appeared in D&D:** 2020
**Originally From:** Magic: The Gathering (2013)
**Tone:** Mythic Fantasy, Greek Epic, Heroic Journey

### Overview

Theros is a world inspired by Greek mythology, adapted from Magic: The Gathering for D&D play. It is a world where gods are real and active, walking among mortals and demanding devotion. Heroes undertake epic odysseys, battle mythic monsters, and can earn or lose the favor of the gods. The setting emphasizes personal heroism, divine relationships, and mythic storytelling.

Unlike most D&D settings, Theros has a strong piety system where a character's relationship with their chosen god is mechanically tracked and rewarded.

### The Gods of Theros

The gods of Theros reside in Nyx, the night sky, and are shaped by mortal belief:

- **Heliod (Sun, Law):** God of the sun, akin to Zeus/Apollo. Arrogant and demanding of worship.
- **Thassa (Sea, Knowledge):** God of the sea, secretive and inscrutable.
- **Erebos (Death, Underworld):** God of the dead, envious of the living.
- **Purphoros (Forge, Fire):** God of the forge, creative and destructive.
- **Nylea (Hunt, Nature):** God of the hunt and wilderness, protector of nature.
- **Iroas (Victory, Honor):** God of victory in war, twin of Mogis.
- **Mogis (Slaughter, War):** God of wanton destruction in war, twin of Iroas.
- **Ephara (Cities, Civilization):** God of the polis and civic life.
- **Phenax (Deception, Trickery):** God of cheats and the Returned (undead).
- **Karametra (Harvest, Home):** God of the harvest and hearth.
- **Athreos (Passage, Death):** The ferryman god who guides the dead to the Underworld.
- **Keranos (Storms, Inspiration):** God of storms and sudden insight.
- **Pharika (Poison, Medicine):** God of affliction and remedy.
- **Klothys (Destiny, Fate):** The eldest god, who weaves the strands of fate.
- **Kruphix (Horizons, Mysteries):** The enigmatic god of the unknown.

### Major Locations

- **Meletis:** A coastal polis of learning and philosophy, akin to Athens.
- **Akros:** A mountain fortress-city of warriors, akin to Sparta.
- **Setessa:** A forest city ruled by a matriarchy, devoted to Nylea and Karametra.
- **The Underworld:** Where the dead go, ruled by Erebos. The Returned are those who escape.
- **Nyx:** The enchanted night sky where the gods reside, visible to all as constellations.
- **The Siren Sea:** A treacherous ocean full of monsters and adventure.

### Unique Mechanics

- **Piety System:** Characters earn piety with their chosen god by performing deeds aligned with that god's ideals. At certain piety thresholds (3, 10, 25, 50), characters gain supernatural boons. Piety can also be lost through actions that displease the god.
- **Supernatural Gifts:** At character creation, each PC receives a supernatural gift (such as being an oracle, a hero destined by prophecy, or someone who returned from the Underworld).
- **Mythic Monsters:** Creatures like the hydra, the minotaur, and the kraken receive mythic traits — additional phases and abilities that activate when they are first "defeated."

### Unique Races

- **Leonin:** Lion-folk who distrust the gods.
- **Satyr:** Mischievous, fun-loving fey creatures.
- **Triton:** Aquatic people of the deep.
- **Centaur:** Inhabitants of the wild lands.
- **Minotaur:** Badlands-dwelling warriors.

### The Returned

Those who escape the Underworld return as the Returned — wearing golden masks, having lost their identity and most of their memories. They are neither fully alive nor fully dead, and are often feared and shunned.

### Published 5e Material

- *Mythic Odysseys of Theros* (2020)

### What Makes It Unique

Theros provides a tightly focused mythological experience. The piety system creates a dynamic, personal relationship between each PC and their chosen god. The mythic monster rules (which were adopted into later 5e products) make boss fights more dramatic. The setting is excellent for campaigns focused on heroic journeys, divine quests, and stories where the gods take an active interest in mortal affairs.

metadata_json.creator: Magic: The Gathering team (D&D adaptation by James Wyatt)

title: Wildemount
summary: The Critical Role campaign setting by Matthew Mercer, featuring warring empires, the mysterious power of dunamancy, and a rich world shaped by live-play storytelling.
content:
## Wildemount

**Also Known As:** Exandria (the world), Wildemount (the continent)
**Created By:** Matthew Mercer
**First Appeared:** 2020 (book), 2018 (Critical Role Campaign 2)
**Tone:** Character-Driven Fantasy, Political Intrigue, Exploration, War

### Overview

Wildemount is the continent where Critical Role's second campaign took place. It is part of the larger world of Exandria, created by Dungeon Master Matthew Mercer. The setting blends classic D&D fantasy with a strong emphasis on character-driven storytelling, moral complexity, and the consequences of war.

The *Explorer's Guide to Wildemount* brought Mercer's homebrew setting to official D&D publication, introducing new subclasses, spells, and the unique magic system of dunamancy.

### Major Regions

- **The Dwendalian Empire:** A large, authoritarian human empire that controls much of western Wildemount. Magic is tightly regulated by the Cerberus Assembly.
- **The Kryn Dynasty:** A nation of drow (dark elves) who worship the Luxon, a deity of light and rebirth. They practice consecution — a form of reincarnation that allows souls to be reborn with memories of past lives.
- **The Menagerie Coast:** A tropical coastline of independent city-states, the Clovis Concord, and pirates. More cosmopolitan and free than the Empire.
- **Eiselcross:** A frozen northern wasteland containing the ruins of Aeor, an ancient flying city destroyed by the gods.
- **Xhorhas:** The eastern wastes, home to the Kryn Dynasty and various monstrous civilizations.
- **Blightshore:** A corrupted, magically blasted region at the eastern edge of the continent.

### Major Cities

- **Rexxentrum:** Capital of the Dwendalian Empire, seat of King Dwendal and the Cerberus Assembly.
- **Rosohna (Ghor Dranas):** Capital of the Kryn Dynasty, a city of perpetual twilight illuminated by magical lights.
- **Nicodranas:** A major port city on the Menagerie Coast.
- **Zadash:** The second-largest city in the Dwendalian Empire.
- **Uthodurn:** A city of dwarves and elves hidden in the mountains.

### Dunamancy

Wildemount's most significant mechanical contribution to D&D. Dunamancy is a school of magic based on manipulating time, gravity, and possibility:

- **Chronurgy Magic (Wizard subclass):** Manipulate time — slow enemies, speed allies, freeze moments.
- **Graviturgy Magic (Wizard subclass):** Control gravity — crush foes, alter weight, create gravity wells.
- **New Spells:** *Gift of Alacrity* (bonus to initiative), *Fortune's Favor* (store a lucky die roll), *Temporal Shunt* (force a creature to vanish and reappear next turn), *Reality Break* (tear apart reality around a creature), *Ravenous Void* (create a black hole).

### Key Organizations

- **Cerberus Assembly:** A council of powerful mages who control magic use in the Dwendalian Empire. Corrupt and ambitious.
- **The Cobalt Soul:** Monks devoted to preserving knowledge and exposing corruption.
- **The Clovis Concord:** A confederation of city-states governing the Menagerie Coast.
- **The Children of Malice:** Followers of Lolth among the Kryn.
- **The Revelry:** Pirates and free-spirits who resist the Concord's authority.

### Key NPCs (from the book, not the show)

- **King Bertrand Dwendal:** The aging, paranoid ruler of the Dwendalian Empire.
- **Bright Queen Leylas Kryn:** The immortal (through consecution) ruler of the Kryn Dynasty.
- **Trent Ikithon:** The sinister head of the Cerberus Assembly's Scourger program.
- **Lady Vess DeRogna:** A Cerberus Assembly member obsessed with the ruins of Aeor.

### Vestiges of Divergence

Exandria features unique magic items called Vestiges of Divergence — legendary artifacts that grow in power as their wielder achieves great deeds. They have three states:
- **Dormant:** Basic magical properties.
- **Awakened:** Enhanced abilities unlocked through heroic acts.
- **Exalted:** Full power realized through epic achievements.

### Published 5e Material

- *Explorer's Guide to Wildemount* (2020)
- *Critical Role: Call of the Netherdeep* (2022, adventure set in Exandria)
- *Tal'Dorei Reborn* (2022, DMs Guild, covers the other Exandria continent)

### What Makes It Unique

Wildemount is a setting born from actual play — it has the depth and lived-in quality that comes from thousands of hours of collaborative storytelling. The war between the Dwendalian Empire and the Kryn Dynasty provides a strong central conflict where neither side is purely good or evil. Dunamancy adds genuinely new mechanical options to D&D. And for Critical Role fans, adventuring in the same world as Vox Machina and the Mighty Nein carries a special thrill.
